finding_title: REQ017-020 absent user stories—four critical regulatory requirements have no acceptance criteria
what_is_missing: REQ017 (Public Stakeholder Engagement), REQ018 (Security Classification Handling), REQ019 (Environmental Impact Assessment), and REQ020 (Post-Trial Close-out) are listed as notes only, not user stories. These four requirements lack: actor/role, acceptance criteria, verification method, and success definition. They are implied by external standards (CAP 1616, GDPR, CAP 722H, JSP 440) but not explicitly specified in system requirements.
where_expected: Each of REQ017-020 should contain a complete user story in the format: 'As a [role] I want [feature] so that [benefit]' with explicit acceptance criteria and success metrics.
impact: Without user stories, system designers cannot know what features to build. Verification testers cannot know how to test. Regulators cannot know when requirements are satisfied. Trial may proceed without: community engagement, classified evidence handling, environmental assessment, or post-trial archival—creating regulatory, legal, and ecological liability.
reasoning: REQ017-020 are explicitly flagged as absent in the requirements source document. Dorothy's glossary, Flo's features analysis, Olivia's outcomes analysis, and Sophia's scenarios analysis all confirm these four are missing formal requirement specification. Alexa's ambiguity analysis confirms REQ017-020 have no user stories. Without user stories, these are aspirations, not requirements.
recommendation: Create explicit user stories for REQ017-020: (1) REQ017: 'As a CAA community liaison I want to manage stakeholder consultation and prior notification per CAP 1616 so that community engagement is documented and GDPR obligations are met.' (2) REQ018: 'As a CAA security officer I want to manage classified MOD evidence per CAP 722H / JSP 440 so that regulatory assurance is enabled without security breach.' (3) REQ019: 'As a CAA environmental assessor I want to assess noise, species disturbance, and battery-fire environmental impact so that ecological compliance is verified.' (4) REQ020: 'As a CAA programme manager I want to formally close authorization with lessons-learned capture and evidence archival so that institutional learning is preserved and regulatory closure is documented.'

finding_title: No cross-cutting authentication / authorisation requirement—role-based access control undefined
what_is_missing: No requirement specifies who can access case files, evidence artefacts, or decision records. Roles are mentioned implicitly (case officer, SORA assessor, DAA assessor, human factors inspector, etc.) but no requirement defines: role definitions, approval authority boundaries, conflict-of-interest restrictions, or access control rules. Example: can a SORA assessor override a DAA assessor's decision? Can applicants access regulator decision drafts?
where_expected: A cross-cutting NFR (non-functional requirement) should define role-based access control (RBAC) or attribute-based access control (ABAC) for all case file access, evidence ingest, and decision logging. This should reference CAA policy on conflict of interest, data protection, and decision authority.
impact: Without role definitions, the system cannot enforce separation of duties (e.g., prevent applicant from viewing regulator's deliberations). Audit trail is compromised if anyone can access evidence without logging. Security is at risk if classified evidence is accessible to all roles.
reasoning: Flo's feature analysis identifies F25 (Operator-Private Feedback vs Regulator Decision Record Segregation) and F24 (Classification-Aware Evidence Segregation) as hidden features, implying role-based access is non-obvious but critical. Dorothy's glossary identifies terms like 'assure' and 'defensibly' that imply decision authority but do not define authority boundaries. No REQ explicitly states access control rules for different user roles.
recommendation: Create explicit cross-cutting requirement: 'As a CAA system administrator I want role-based access control so that case file access is restricted to authorized roles and audit trail is maintained. Roles: [Applicant (read-only to own case), CAA Case Officer (read-write case metadata), SORA Assessor (read-write SORA evidence), DAA Assessor (read-write DAA evidence), Security Officer (read-write classified evidence with redaction), Regulator Panel (read-write decision records), CAA Director (override/escalation authority)]. Access rules: [Applicant cannot view regulator decision drafts], [Classified evidence viewable only by security-cleared staff], [All access logged with timestamp/actor]. Implement per [ISO/IEC 27001 access control] or CAA policy.'

finding_title: No audit logging requirement—system actions (approval, rejection, override) not traceable for forensic review
what_is_missing: No requirement specifies that system actions (case opening, document upload, assessment approval, gate reversal, decision override) are logged with timestamp, actor, and change details. Olivia's outcomes analysis identifies F30 (Audit Logging & Forensic Trail) as a hidden feature; Flo identifies F26 (Panel Override & Decision Logging) as hidden. Neither is explicitly required.
where_expected: A cross-cutting NFR should define audit logging for all state changes, decisions, and approvals. Examples: 'Case opened by officer X at 14:32, SORA assessment approved by assessor Y at 15:15, gate reversal decision made by panel Z at 16:00 due to [incident/evidence gap].' Audit log should be immutable and legally discoverable.
impact: Without audit logging, the system cannot defend decisions in case of legal challenge or accident investigation. Regulator cannot verify that assessments followed required procedures. Hidden errors (wrong assessment approved, evidence tampered with) cannot be detected. Trial may proceed with non-compliant authorization and no forensic trail.
reasoning: Olivia's outcomes analysis explicitly names F30 (Audit Logging & Forensic Trail) as an 'implied by incident investigation (F13) and decision logging (F26)' but notes it is not explicit. Multiple analyses mention decision logging (Olivia) and reversible gates (Flo, Sophia) but no REQ specifies how reversals are logged or how audit trail is maintained. Dorothy's findings on 'assure' and accountability note that passive voice obscures responsibility.
recommendation: Create explicit cross-cutting requirement: 'As a CAA auditor I want all system actions logged so that authorization decisions are forensically reviewable and audit trail is legally defensible. Audit log shall capture: [action type], [actor/role], [timestamp], [affected artefact], [change details], [justification/reason]. Immutability: logs cannot be modified post-creation; deletion only per [retention policy]. Access: audit log visible to [CAA management, external auditors, accident investigators] with [read-only | audit trail summary only] permissions. Retention: [minimum 7 years post-authorization | per MOR investigation retention]. Example: 'SORA_APPROVED by assessor john.doe@caa.gov.uk at 2025-03-15T14:32:00Z for case TRIAL-001, evidence set: [SORA_v2_swarm_aware.pdf, risk_matrix.xlsx].'

finding_title: No data validation / data entry rules requirement—artefact quality not enforced
what_is_missing: No requirement specifies validation rules for data entry or artefact upload. Examples: ConOps document must include sections A, B, C; SORA evidence must have risk matrix in format X; insurance certificate must be in PDF; geofence must be defined as polygon with [min X, max X, min Y, max Y]. Without validation rules, applicants can submit incomplete or non-conformant artefacts that system accepts but reviewers reject, creating rework and delay.
where_expected: A cross-cutting NFR should define data validation for each artefact type. Examples: ConOps validation rule: 'ConOps document must be >= 10 pages, include sections for [nominal procedures, contingency procedures, crew roles, decision triggers], and define all acronyms on first use.' Insurance validation: 'Certificate must be PDF, from approved insurer, coverage >= £10M, exclusions documented, valid for [duration of trial + 1 year].'
impact: Without validation rules, quality control is manual and subjective. Applicants receive inconsistent feedback on submission acceptability. Case officer spends time re-requesting missing information. Early validation catches errors before formal review, saving time and frustration.
reasoning: REQ001 (F02 Completeness Check) requires validation but does not define validation rules beyond 'complete'. Flo's features analysis identifies validation as part of F02 but notes 'what constitutes complete is undefined'. Alexa's ambiguity analysis notes 'complete pack is undefined; no artefact enumeration.' Without explicit validation rules, system cannot automate completeness check.
recommendation: Define explicit data validation requirement: 'As a system validator I want to enforce data entry rules so that submitted artefacts conform to CAA standards and reduce rework. Validation rules per artefact: (1) ConOps: [format=PDF, size>10 pages, must include sections: [Nominal Procedures, Contingencies, Crew Roles, Decision Trees], acronyms defined on first use]. (2) ODD: [format=structured table or spreadsheet, must define: [geographic bounds (lat/lon), altitude range, environmental limits (wind, visibility, temperature)]]. (3) SORA evidence: [must include JARUS SORA v2.5 risk matrix, hazard assessment, mitigation evidence per [structured table]]. (4) Insurance: [must be PDF from [approved insurer list], coverage amount >= £10M, exclusions documented, valid dates overlapping trial period]. (5) Geofence: [format=KML or [proprietary format], must be closed polygon, all points defined with [latitude, longitude, altitude, tolerance band]]. Validation: [automated via schema / manual checklist]. Feedback on failure: [specific violation reported to applicant].'

finding_title: REQ016 phased gates lack explicit pass/fail criteria—evidence thresholds undefined
what_is_missing: REQ016 requires 'phased gates' for progressive build-up but does not specify gate entry criteria or approval authority. Examples: 'What constitutes sufficient evidence to pass Phase 1 gate?' Is it [zero incidents in N flights]? [Swarm behavior nominal in all autonomy modes]? [Both]? Who approves gate progression? [CAA manager alone]? [With SORA expert review]? [With independent panel]? Sophia's scenario analysis includes data-driven gate criteria (SCN-060) but this is not explicitly required. Olivia's outcomes analysis notes 'phase gate progression decision criteria undefined' as major finding.
where_expected: REQ016 should specify gate criteria for each phase: 'Phase 1 gate (single aircraft, VLOS/EVLOS): pass criteria are [10 incident-free flights, ConOps nominal procedures validated]. Phase 2 gate (single aircraft, BVLOS): pass criteria are [15 incident-free flights, autonomy behavior nominal per ODD bounds]. ... Phase approval authority: [CAA programme manager with SORA expert review]; decision documented in [gate review report]; timeline [10 days] after evidence submission.'
impact: Without explicit gate criteria, phase progression is subjective. One applicant's evidence may be deemed sufficient; another's identical evidence may be insufficient. Gate approval becomes political (depends on assessor judgment) rather than objective (criteria met or not). Trial timeline is unpredictable because gate decision timeframe is unknown.
reasoning: Sophia's scenarios analysis includes SCN-060 (phased trial gates with evidence type matrices) showing gate structure but notes this is data-driven (not a requirement). Olivia's outcomes analysis identifies 'phase gate progression decision criteria undefined' as major finding. Flo's features analysis notes 'phase gates lack explicit pass/fail criteria.' Multiple analysts identify the gap but no explicit REQ defines gate criteria.
recommendation: Define explicit gate criteria in REQ016: 'For each phase gate, define entry criteria, pass metrics, and approval process: Phase 1 (single aircraft, VLOS/EVLOS): [Entry: completeness check pass. Pass metrics: 10 incident-free flights, ConOps nominal procedures demonstrated, crew competency validated. Approval: CAA programme manager with safety review.] Phase 2 (single aircraft, BVLOS): [Entry: Phase 1 pass. Pass metrics: 15 BVLOS flights, autonomy behavior nominal per ODD bounds, geofence integrity confirmed, link-loss contingency tested. Approval: CAA programme manager + SORA expert.] Phase 3 (multi-aircraft, coordination): [Entry: Phase 2 pass. Pass metrics: 10 coordinated flights, swarm de-confliction tested, inter-vehicle link failure handled, emergent behavior benign. Approval: CAA programme manager + autonomy expert.] Phase 4 (full trial envelope): [Entry: Phase 3 pass. Pass metrics: 20 full-envelope flights, all contingencies tested, no unplanned terminations. Approval: CAA panel (director + 2 experts)]. Gate decision timeline: [10 working days] after evidence submission. Gate approval document: [gate review report signed by all reviewers] filed in case record.'

finding_title: No regulator training / onboarding requirement—assessor competency not defined
what_is_missing: No requirement specifies qualifications or training for CAA assessors (SORA assessor, DAA assessor, autonomy reviewer, human factors inspector, environmental assessor). Implied qualifications (expert in their domain) are never formalized. No requirement addresses: how are assessors trained on swarm-specific risks? How is JARUS SORA v2.5 expertise ensured? How are conflicts of interest managed?
where_expected: A cross-cutting requirement should define assessor competency: 'Each CAA assessment role shall be performed by a person with: [domain expertise (education + experience)], [training on CAA processes and this trial], [no conflict of interest with applicant].' Example: 'SORA assessor shall have: [minimum X years aviation safety experience], [completion of JARUS SORA v2.5 training course], [independent of trial operator].'
impact: Without assessor qualification requirement, assessment quality is inconsistent. New CAA staff may not understand swarm-specific risks. Assessment may be biased if assessor has financial relationship with applicant. Future trials may repeat mistakes from prior trials if lessons-learned are not formalized in training.
reasoning: REQ010 specifies 'CAA human factors inspector' and other roles but does not define their qualifications. Flo's features analysis notes role definitions are implied but not explicit. No analyst mentioned assessor training as a gap, but absence of training requirement in a complex domain (BVLOS swarm autonomy) is notable.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'As a CAA training director I want to ensure assessor competency so that assessments are consistent and of high quality. For each assessment role, define: (1) SORA assessor: [minimum 5 years aviation safety experience, completion of JARUS SORA v2.5 course (annually updated), certification on swarm-specific risk assessment (new internal training)]. (2) DAA assessor: [minimum 3 years DAA or collision avoidance experience, certification on DO-365 and F3442 standards, swarm encounter scenarios (new internal training)]. (3) Autonomy reviewer: [minimum 5 years autonomous systems experience, training on distributed decision-making and emergent behavior analysis]. (4) Environmental assessor: [certification in environmental impact assessment, noise modeling, wildlife impact analysis]. (5) Conflict of interest: assessor cannot work for / have financial interest in / be related to applicant. Assessor training shall be updated annually to reflect lessons-learned from completed trials.'

finding_title: No Workbench assurance requirement—system itself is not tested / assured
what_is_missing: The system (Workbench) that manages BVLOS authorization cases is not covered by requirements. System risks are: [bugs that corrupt case data], [false positive/negative completeness checks], [audit trail tampering], [unauthorized access]. Yet no requirement addresses: testing strategy for the system, version control, change management, backup/disaster recovery, or post-deployment monitoring. The Workbench itself could fail and invalidate all case authorizations.
where_expected: A meta-requirement should address Workbench assurance: 'As a CAA IT manager I want the Workbench system to be assured so that case data integrity and decision defensibility are maintained. The system shall meet: [availability SLA 99.9% | redundancy and backup | incident response plan | version control for all configuration / rule changes | pre-deployment testing per [development standard] | post-deployment monitoring for [correctness, performance, security]].'
impact: If Workbench fails (data corruption, security breach, availability loss), all pending cases are affected. Regulators lose case history and audit trail. In accident investigation, opposing counsel may argue authorization decision is not defensible because system integrity is questioned. Post-deployment bugs may silently change gate logic or allow unauthorized users to access classified evidence.
reasoning: REQs focus on BVLOS trial authorization but not on the system that manages authorizations. No analyst explicitly mentioned Workbench assurance. However, this is a classic 'assurance of the tool' meta-problem: the tool itself (Workbench) must be assured with same rigor as the trial it manages.
recommendation: Add meta-requirement: 'As a CAA system owner I want the Workbench system to be assured so that authorization decisions are legally defensible. Workbench shall meet: (1) [Availability]: SLA 99.9% uptime; failover to [backup system] within [5 minutes] of failure. (2) [Data integrity]: [nightly backup], [backup tested monthly], [disaster recovery plan tested annually]. (3) [Change control]: all configuration and rule changes tracked in [version control]; changes approved by [CAA manager + technical review] before deployment. (4) [Testing]: pre-deployment testing per [ISO/IEC 25010 software quality model], including [functional testing, integration testing, regression testing, performance testing]. (5) [Security]: access control per [role-based access control], [audit logging of all access], [encryption of data at rest and in transit]. (6) [Monitoring]: post-deployment monitoring for [system availability, performance (latency, throughput), security incidents (unauthorized access attempts), audit log integrity]. (7) [Incident response]: [on-call support team], [incident response plan], [post-incident review and lessons learned]. (8) [Documentation]: system design documentation, change log, known issues list maintained in [version control].'

finding_title: No inter-version clause-library migration requirement—how old cases are re-assessed under new CAA guidance
what_is_missing: BVLOS swarm trials may extend over 2-3 years. During that time, CAA guidance (CAP 722H, CAP 1616) may be updated, causing interpretation changes or new requirements. No requirement specifies how ongoing authorizations handle guidance updates: (1) Are old authorizations grandfathered under prior guidance? (2) Must applicants re-assess under new guidance? (3) If new guidance imposes stricter safety standards, can ongoing trials be required to comply? How are conflicts resolved?
where_expected: A requirement should address clause versioning: 'As a CAA case officer I want regulatory clause versions to be pinned to case authorization so that future re-assessment is clear when guidance changes. When CAA guidance (CAP 722H, etc.) is updated, the system shall: [1] mark applicable cases for re-assessment, [2] notify applicant of new guidance, [3] set timeline for re-assessment per [policy], [4] generate re-assessment report showing compliance with new guidance.'
impact: Without guidance versioning, applicant may be uncertain whether ongoing trial is still compliant after CAA updates guidance. Regulator may be liable if an incident occurs and new guidance would have prevented it. Case file loses clarity about which guidance version applied to the authorization decision.
reasoning: Flo's features analysis identifies F23 (Clause-Library Version Pinning & Management) as a hidden feature but notes it is 'not explicitly requested; implied by need for repeatable regulatory interpretation.' No REQ explicitly addresses version management for guidance documents.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'As a CAA case officer I want clause library versions pinned to authorization so that case interpretation is reproducible and re-assessment procedures are clear. System shall: (1) Record the version/date of all regulatory clauses (CAP 722H, CAP 1616, etc.) at time of authorization. (2) When a clause is updated, mark all cases pinned to prior version as [eligible for re-assessment]. (3) Notification process: [applicant notified within 30 days of clause update], offered choice to [maintain authorization under prior clause version OR re-assess under new version]. (4) Re-assessment timeline: [60 days] for material changes, [annual review] for minor updates. (5) Conflict resolution: if new guidance imposes stricter safety requirements, phased in per [policy] (e.g., new clause applies to [ongoing trials or new trials only]). (6) Audit trail: case file shows which clause version was used for authorization decision.'

finding_title: No requirement for case appeal / re-opening—how denied authorizations can be challenged
what_is_missing: If CAA denies authorization (insufficient evidence, unacceptable risk, non-compliance with clause), no requirement specifies applicant's right to appeal or re-submit. Can applicant submit new evidence and request re-review? Is there a deadline for re-submission? Is appeal to independent body (not the original assessor) possible? Without appeal mechanism, applicant's only option is legal challenge (court action), which is expensive and slow.
where_expected: A feature should address appeal/re-opening: 'As an applicant I want to appeal a rejected authorization or re-open a case with new evidence so that I can challenge regulatory decisions and remediate issues identified in rejection.'
impact: Without appeal mechanism, applicant has no path forward after rejection except litigation. Regulator has finality but may make errors (misunderstand evidence, apply wrong standard). Industry (potential BVLOS operators) may be discouraged if they perceive regulatory decisions are unfair or unchangeable.
reasoning: No analyst explicitly mentioned appeal mechanism. REQ016 (phased gates) includes reversal (phase can be rolled back) but this is different from appeal (challenging original decision, not rolling back progression). No REQ addresses post-denial remediation.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'As an applicant I want to appeal a rejected authorization so that I can remediate issues and resubmit without restarting the entire process. Appeal process: (1) Rejection decision includes [specific findings], [reasons for denial], [opportunities to remediate]. (2) Applicant has [60 days] to file [appeal request with new evidence | re-submission plan] addressing specific findings. (3) Re-review conducted by [different assessor | assessor + independent expert], timeline [30 days]. (4) Re-review may result in [APPROVED (remediation successful)], [CONDITIONAL (approved if applicant meets conditions)], or [DENIED (no remediation possible)]. (5) Final decision is documented in case file with appeal history. (6) Applicant may request [mediation | independent panel review] if still disagreeing with re-review decision.'

finding_title: No cross-tool interoperability requirement—Workbench integration with airspace change tools, AAIB, Service Inquiry undefined
what_is_missing: BVLOS trials may involve multiple regulatory tools: [TDA coordination with airspace change system], [post-incident reporting to AAIB or Service Inquiry], [case data export to regulatory audit system]. No requirement specifies how Workbench integrates with these tools or exchanges data. Example: does TDA approval in airspace system automatically update case status in Workbench? Does incident investigation in AAIB system reference case authorization from Workbench?
where_expected: A cross-cutting requirement should address interoperability: 'As a CAA systems administrator I want Workbench to integrate with [airspace change system | AAIB | audit system] so that trial authorization and oversight are coordinated across systems. Integration shall include: [data exchange format (e.g., XML, API)], [synchronization frequency], [conflict resolution if systems disagree].'
impact: Without interoperability, Workbench is an isolated system. Data is manually re-entered into other systems, creating inconsistency and error. Regulators must check multiple systems to understand full trial status. Incidents may not be linked back to originating authorization (AAIB investigates incident but doesn't have Workbench context).
reasoning: REQ004 (TDA coordination) and REQ012 (MOR/incident reporting) imply integration with external systems but don't specify mechanism. Flo's features analysis notes no hidden features for interoperability. No analyst mentioned cross-tool integration.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'As a CAA systems administrator I want Workbench to integrate with external regulatory systems so that trial lifecycle is coordinated. Integration points: (1) TDA / airspace change system: [when TDA approved in airspace system, status automatically updates in Workbench]. (2) AAIB incident system: [when incident reported to AAIB, Workbench case is linked and marked with incident reference]. (3) CAA audit system: [Workbench exports case summary (applicant, authorization date, SAIL/OSO, conditions) for audit trail]. (4) Crew licensing system: [crew credentials verified against CAA license database; updates to crew credentials trigger case review if competency changes]. Data format: [JSON API | XML / EDI], frequency: [real-time | daily batch sync], conflict resolution: [flag inconsistencies for manual review].'
